title: Colour Theory Foundations — Hue, Saturation, Lightness
content: The HSL model (Hue, Saturation, Lightness) is the most intuitive for UI design. Hue: the colour family (0–360° on the colour wheel). Saturation: intensity from grey (0%) to vivid (100%). Lightness: brightness from black (0%) to white (100%). The HSB model (Hue, Saturation, Brightness) is used in most design tools. Key insight: saturation and lightness interact — highly saturated dark colours appear almost black and lose their hue identity. For UI colour systems, work in HSL to maintain consistent perceived brightness across hues. Never trust RGB sliders for colour harmony — the numbers don't reflect perceptual relationships.

title: Colour Harmonies — Relationships That Work
content: Classic colour harmonies: Monochromatic (single hue, varied lightness/saturation) — cohesive, sophisticated, elegant. Complementary (opposite hues: blue/orange, red/green) — high contrast, vibrant, use as accent pairs not equal areas. Analogous (adjacent hues: blue/blue-green/green) — harmonious, natural, low tension. Split-complementary (one hue + two adjacent to its complement) — vibrant but balanced, safer than pure complementary. Triadic (three equally spaced hues) — energetic, complex, requires careful balance. Tetradic/square (four hues) — richest but hardest to balance. In UI practice: monochromatic or analogous for backgrounds/surfaces + one complementary accent for CTAs is the most reliable approach.

title: Colour Temperature — Warm, Cool, and Neutral
content: Colour temperature affects perception of space, time, and emotion. Warm colours (reds, oranges, yellows) — advance visually (appear closer), energetic, urgent, appetising, associated with heat and action. Cool colours (blues, greens, purples) — recede visually (appear further away), calming, professional, trustworthy, associated with water, sky, technology. Neutral colours (greys, beiges, taupes) — take on temperature from adjacent colours. Practical UI applications: use warm accents for urgency (CTAs, alerts), cool palettes for trust-critical products (finance, healthcare), warm backgrounds for comfort (food, hospitality). Temperature also affects perceived contrast — warm and cool colours at the same lightness can appear to vibrate when adjacent.

title: Colour Contrast for Accessibility and Readability
content: WCAG 2.1 contrast ratios: AA = 4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text (18pt+) and UI components. AAA = 7:1 for normal text. These use the WCAG relative luminance formula — not just brightness. Practical checker tools: Figma plugin Contrast, A11y Colour Contrast Checker, Colour & Contrast. Important: Colour alone cannot convey meaning (colour blindness affects ~8% of men). Always pair colour with shape, icon, or text. The most common contrast failure: grey text on white backgrounds — designers assume it 'looks fine' at 1× on their retina screen but fails at standard brightness. Tip: check contrast with your screen at 50% brightness to simulate real-world conditions.

title: Simultaneous Contrast — How Colours Change Each Other
content: A colour looks different depending on what surrounds it. The same grey appears lighter on black than on white. The same orange appears more vibrant next to blue than next to red. This is simultaneous contrast — first described by Chevreul in 1839 and the foundation of Impressionist technique. UI implications: colours in your palette that look fine in isolation may clash or shift in context. Always evaluate colours in their actual layout context, never swatches. Dark mode: colours that pass contrast checks on white may not on dark backgrounds and vice versa — every colour token needs separate light/dark values. Background colour directly affects perceived saturation of foreground elements.

title: Colour Psychology — What Each Hue Communicates
content: Hue associations (Western context, vary by culture): Red — urgency, danger, passion, error, appetite. Strongest attention-getter. Use sparingly. Orange — warmth, energy, creativity, friendliness, affordability. Blue — trust, stability, professionalism, calm, technology. Most universally liked. Green — nature, growth, health, success, money. Purple — luxury, creativity, mystery, spirituality. Yellow — optimism, warmth, caution, attention. Trickiest for accessibility. Black — sophistication, power, luxury, formality. White — cleanliness, simplicity, space, purity. These are tendencies, not rules — context and culture always override. A red CTA doesn't mean danger if everything else on the page signals safety.

title: Colour Systems — Primitives, Semantics, and Components
content: A mature colour system has three layers. Layer 1 — Primitive/global tokens: raw colour values (blue-500 = #3B82F6). 8–12 shades per hue, 50–900 scale. These are never used directly in components. Layer 2 — Semantic tokens: meaning-based aliases (colour-action-primary = blue-500, colour-feedback-error = red-600). These are what components consume. Changing light/dark mode means remapping semantic tokens to different primitives. Layer 3 — Component tokens: component-specific (button-primary-bg, input-border-focus). These point to semantic tokens. This hierarchy allows theming, white-labeling, and dark mode without touching component code.

title: Building a UI Colour Palette — Steps and Principles
content: Steps to build a robust UI palette: 1. Choose a brand primary hue. 2. Build a 9-step scale (50–900) using a curve: 50 is near-white, 900 is near-black, 500 is the 'hero' colour. Adjust saturation — lighter shades should be less saturated, not just lighter. 3. Build a neutral scale (grey) — either cool-tinted (blue-grey) or warm-tinted (warm grey) depending on brand temperature. 4. Establish semantic colours: success (green), warning (amber), error (red), info (blue) — these must all work on white AND dark backgrounds. 5. Define surface colours: background, surface, elevated surface, overlay. 6. Validate every combination for WCAG AA. Common mistake: creating a palette that looks good on artboards but fails in actual UI where colours appear adjacent.

title: Colour in Dark Mode — More Than Just Inversion
content: Dark mode is not colour inversion. True dark mode principles: don't use pure black (#000000) — use very dark grey (e.g. #0F0F0F or #1A1A1A) to reduce eye strain. Elevation in dark mode is shown by lightening surfaces, not adding shadows. Saturated colours need to be toned down 15–25% in dark mode — vivid colours feel harsh against dark backgrounds. Text on dark: pure white is too harsh — use off-white (95–98% lightness). Colour meanings shift in dark: a grey that signals 'disabled' in light mode may appear 'active' in dark. Every semantic token needs a dark-mode value. Google Material 3 and Apple HIG both publish detailed dark mode colour guidance.

title: Colour and Light — Physical Principles Applied to UI
content: Understanding light physics makes UI colour decisions more intuitive. Key principles: light colours are additive (RGB screens add light — maximum is white, minimum is black). Shadow = absence of light, not presence of dark colour. Surfaces facing a light source are warm (yellowish), shadow sides are cool (bluish). Ambient light tints everything slightly. These principles underpin realistic UI lighting: elevation shadows should be cool and transparent, not opaque dark grey. Highlight a button's top surface slightly warmer than the base colour. Neumorphism took this too literally (failed because the metaphor broke at scale), but the underlying physics are still valid for micro-shadow work in cards, inputs, and icon design.

title: Colour in Branding — Owning a Colour
content: Colour is the most powerful brand recognition signal — faster than shape, faster than text. Studies show colour alone can increase brand recognition by up to 80%. Owning a colour requires: consistent use across all touchpoints, a distinctive enough colour to differentiate (Tiffany's robin egg blue, Barbie pink, UPS brown). For UI: the brand primary colour doesn't have to be the most dominant — it's often most powerful as an accent used sparingly against neutral backgrounds. The most common brand colour mistake in UI: making everything the brand colour and losing contrast hierarchy. Cadence matters: 60% neutral, 30% secondary, 10% primary accent is a reliable starting ratio.

title: Colour Across Cultures — Global UI Considerations
content: Colour meanings are not universal. Key differences: White — purity/wedding (Western), mourning/death (China, Japan, parts of India). Red — danger/stop (Western), luck/prosperity/celebration (China). Green — go/safe (Western), jealousy (France), religion (Islam — use carefully in Middle East product design). Purple — royalty (Western), mourning (Brazil, Thailand). Yellow — caution (Western), sacred (Buddhist contexts), mourning (Egypt). Blue — near-universally positive but meanings vary. Design consideration: localise colour choices for products serving global markets. Never use a culturally sensitive colour combination (e.g. orange + green can be politically charged in some contexts) without local review.
